questions.question: Explain the Spark architecture including Driver, Executors, and Cluster Manager. How does it execute a Spark application?
keyPoints: Driver Program: Main control containing SparkContext, converts user code to tasks, schedules on executors, collects results.
Cluster Manager: Allocates resources across the cluster (YARN, Mesos, Kubernetes), manages worker nodes.
Executors: Processes on worker nodes that run tasks in threads, store data in cache, communicate with driver.
Execution flow: Driver creates SparkContext, requests resources from Cluster Manager, launches executors on workers, sends tasks, executors process and return results.

questions.question: Describe RDDs in Apache Spark. Explain transformations vs actions, lazy evaluation, and fault tolerance. Provide an IoT data processing example.
keyPoints: RDD Definition: Immutable, distributed collection processed in parallel; fundamental Spark abstraction with three properties: Resilient, Distributed, Immutable.
Transformations: Lazy operations creating new RDDs (map, filter, flatMap); build a DAG without execution.
Actions: Eager operations triggering computation (count, collect, reduce); return results to driver.
Lazy Evaluation: Transformations deferred until action called; enables optimization, efficient pipelining, avoids unnecessary computation.
Fault Tolerance: Lineage-based recovery; RDD remembers transformation sequence; lost partitions recomputed automatically from parent RDDs.
Persistence: cache() or persist() stores RDD in memory/disk for reuse across multiple actions.
IoT Example: Read sensor CSV -> filter valid readings (transformation) -> map to (sensor_id, temp) -> compute average per sensor -> collect() triggers execution.
Performance: In-memory caching makes iterative operations 10-100x faster than MapReduce for IoT analytics.

questions.question: Compare Apache Spark with Hadoop MapReduce for IoT data analytics.
keyPoints: Performance: Spark is 10-100x faster due to in-memory processing; MapReduce uses disk I/O between stages.
Processing model: Spark supports batch, streaming, ML, and graph on one platform; MapReduce is batch-only.
Ease of use: Spark has concise APIs in Python/Scala/Java; MapReduce requires verbose boilerplate code.
Real-time: Spark Streaming provides near real-time via micro-batches; MapReduce has minutes-level latency.
When to use MapReduce: Very large datasets where memory is insufficient, simple ETL jobs, existing MapReduce infrastructure.

questions.question: Explain Spark Streaming and how it enables real-time IoT analytics. Discuss DStreams, micro-batching, and integration with Kafka.
keyPoints: Spark Streaming: Extension of core Spark for processing live data streams using micro-batch architecture.
DStream (Discretized Stream): Continuous series of RDDs, each representing data received during a time interval.
Micro-batching: Live data divided into small intervals (1-5 seconds), each batch processed as an RDD using standard Spark operations.
Kafka integration: KafkaUtils.createDirectStream() reads from Kafka topics, each micro-batch gets a range of Kafka offsets for exactly-once semantics.
IoT pipeline: Kafka receives sensor data -> Spark Streaming reads micro-batches -> applies transformations (parse, filter, aggregate) -> writes results to dashboard/database.
Advantages over true streaming: Code reuse with batch Spark, MLlib integration for online predictions, fault tolerance via RDD lineage.
Limitation: Micro-batch latency (seconds) vs true streaming latency (milliseconds) - Storm is better for sub-second requirements.
